story:
The cold forest kept punching the air out of me as I pushed deeper in, breath stinging, hairs rising on my neck every few steps. I spun around more than once. No one. Still, I didn’t dare use magic. It would flare like a beacon, and my face was already stapled to every tree as a wanted poster. The red wig and sunglasses helped, even if the sharp tang of formaldehyde drifted on the wind. I kept going until the trees thinned and a highway roared into view. A hotel billboard flashed through the bending grass.

I stopped and pulled Damien’s photo from my satchel. He was my fiancé. Twenty-one and engaged, and now I carried him in a rectangle of paper because he was gone. I felt responsible for what happened, even if it was ridiculous to think I’d killed him. With no alibi and a lifetime of being different—of practicing magic—I was the easy suspect. My skin prickled again. I slid the picture away and walked toward the hotel.

The neon sign of a coffee shop winked beside the building, reminding me of Sunday mornings with my mom that had ended the day my life fell apart. Inside the bar, a martini burned a clean, hot trail down my throat. A man slid into the empty space beside me, all smooth smile and wrinkle-free skin. Orlando. He joked about my age; I told him I had ID. He apologized and tried again, asking why I was in town. Work, he said for himself—something he thought would be easy. His blond hair didn’t sit right. I kept seeing a wig no matter how I angled my eyes. He was talkative. Men are, sometimes harmlessly, sometimes not. When he suggested we get a room, I said yes and kept my guard up.

Hours later, the shower hissed behind a bathroom door while I stared at flamingos printed on the curtains. He came out, and the silence stretched a beat too long before I turned. Gun. My real name on his lips—Cecily—like a key he’d been sent to use. That confirmed it: only a government agent would know who I really was. He wanted the glory of killing me. He pulled the trigger. I moved my hand. The bullet slashed through the air, then curved back like it knew where it belonged. It punched into his chest. He fell hard. Red spread across his shirt.

I checked his pulse anyway. Nothing. In his coat pocket, I found a photograph of me. I let fire bloom from my palm—gold, orange, red. It ate the photo first, then rolled over his body until both were gone and the flames winked out cleanly. I bit my lip. Using magic here meant I’d need to move again. New hotel. New name. New wig.

I had walked into that bar to disappear, and instead the hunt had stepped out of a shower with a gun. I hadn’t wanted to use magic, but staying alive demanded it. Damien’s picture pressed against my ribs inside the satchel as I slipped into the night, the world still watching for a girl who wouldn’t stop running.
